figures.caption: Fig. 7: Fisheye reconstruction – Our method reliably removes human distractors and reconstructs high-quality 3D scenes. The table reports PSNR, where our method outperforms both robust and non-robust radiance-field baselines. OLRF renderings are mapped to fisheye frames for qualitative comparison only.

Fig. 12: Ricoh360 dataset – Quantitative comparison on the Ricoh360 dataset [5] together with qualitative results of our method. Our approach achieves higher PSNR ↑ in all scenes and reliably removes the static camera holder. Regions underneath the holder remain empty in the reconstruction, since they are unobserved in the input, rather than producing visible artifacts.

Fig. 1: Teaser – With 360◦ cameras we can recover 3D scenes far more effectively than with traditional perspective cameras, resulting in substantial gains in novel view synthesis (left: perspective vs. 360◦). However, casual 360◦ captures unavoidably include the camera operator, which degrades reconstruction quality if left unaddressed (e.g. 3DGRT [35]). Our FullCircle method overcomes this challenge, enabling fast, high-quality scene capture using 360◦ cameras.

Fig. 6: Effect of undistortion – Using fisheye frames with FullCircle yields higherquality reconstructions, both qualitatively and quantitatively, compared to perspective baselines—robust and non-robust—that operate on preprocessed, undistorted images.

Fig. 8: Camera operator segmentation – We compare our masking approach for removing the camera operator with OmniSAM, a recent segmentation method for omnidirectional images, both qualitatively and quantitatively.
Fig. 9: Synthetic fisheye – Without re-centering the fisheye images synthetically, SAMv2 [37] misses the capturer in some frames, resulting in a noisy reconstruction.
Fig. 5: Dual-fisheye vs. perspective capture – Using dual-fisheye images for both
Fig. 11: Camera operator segmentation – Additional qualitative comparison between OmniSAM and our method for camera operator segmentation.
